evals.prompt: Write an AGENTS.md guide for our Rust repo focused on error handling and concurrent tool execution. The project uses tokio and has multiple agents calling external APIs in parallel.
evals.expected_output: A dense AGENTS.md with: actionable rules for Result/Error types, the ? operator, custom error types via thiserror/anyhow, Arc<Mutex<T>> patterns for shared state, mpsc channels, and tokio::spawn patterns. Should reference Rust Book chapters ch09 and ch16/ch17.
assertions: The output is a Markdown document with clear section headers
The guide covers Result<T, E> and the ? operator for error propagation
The guide includes at least one concrete Rust code snippet
The guide addresses concurrent execution (threads, async, or channels)
The guide avoids generic advice and stays Rust-specific

evals.prompt: I'm setting up a new Rust project. Can you set up the rust-book knowledge base using the Rust book source at /tmp/rust-book/src, then generate an idiomatic Rust style guide covering ownership, traits, and collections for our team?
evals.expected_output: First runs the distiller script (or explains how to if not already done), then uses get_table_of_contents to identify chapters ch04 (ownership), ch08 (collections), ch10 (traits), then generates a style guide grounded in those chapters.
assertions: The agent checks for or attempts to run the distiller script
The agent consults the Table of Contents to identify relevant chapters
The final guide covers ownership/borrowing rules
The final guide covers trait usage patterns
The final guide includes code examples

evals.prompt: Generate a concise Rust best-practices cheat sheet for smart pointers and interior mutability. Format it as a table of when to use Box, Rc, Arc, RefCell, and Mutex.
evals.expected_output: A structured reference document (possibly with a table) covering Box<T>, Rc<T>, Arc<T>, RefCell<T>, and Mutex<T> with use cases and trade-offs, drawn from Rust Book chapter 15.
assertions: The output includes Box<T>, Rc<T>, Arc<T>, RefCell<T>, and Mutex<T>
The output includes a comparison table or structured list of when to use each
The output includes at least one code snippet showing the syntax
The content is Rust-specific and technically accurate
